revision.text:
[[bcl:Chōnin]][[ca:Chonin]][[el:Τσόνιν]][[en:Chōnin]][[es:Chōnin]][[eu:Txonin]][[fa:چونین]][[ff:Chōnin]][[fi:Chōnin]][[fr:Chōnin]][[hu:Csónin]][[id:Chōnin]][[it:Chōnin]][[ja:町人]][[ko:조닌]][[ms:Chōnin]][[pt:Chonin]][[ro:Orășeanul Japoniei medievale]][[ru:Тёнин]][[tr:Chōnin]][[zh:町人]] {{Short description|Social class in Japan|qq=}} {{Italic title|reason=|qq=}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021|qq=}} [[File:Sculpture of a Retired Townsman as a Lay Buddhist, 17th-18th century.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Sculpture of a retired ''chōnin'' as a [[:en:Upāsaka and Upāsikā|lay Buddhist]]. It was common for ''chōnin'' to take up Buddhism after retirement.
{{small|{{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, circa 1700.|qq=}}]] {{nihongo||町人|'''Chōnin'''|extra="townsman"|qq=}} was a social class that emerged in Japan during the early years of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Tokugawa|1=Edo period}} period. In the social hierarchy, it was considered subordinate to the {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} warrior class.{{Cite book|title=Edo Culture: Daily Life and Diversions in Urban Japan, 1600-1868|url=https://archive.org/details/edoculturedailyl00nish|url-access=limited|last=Nishiyama|first=Matsunosuke|date=1997|publisher=University of Hawai'i Press|isbn=0824817362|location=Honolulu|pages=[https://archive.org/details/edoculturedailyl00nish/page/n200 204]|qq=}} ==Social class== [[File:Edo Fukagawa 1.JPG|thumb|Fukagawa, {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} ([[:en:Fukagawa (Tokyo)|Fukagawa]] Edo Museum)]] The ''chōnin'' emerged in ''joka-machi'' or castle towns during the sixteenth century.{{Cite book|title=Japan Encyclopedia|last=Frédéric|first=Louis|date=2002|publisher=Harvard University Press|isbn=0674007700|location=Cambridge, MA|pages=121|qq=}} The majority of ''chōnin'' were merchants, but some were craftsmen. {{nihongo||農民|Nōmin|extra="[[Four_occupations#Nóng_(农/農)|farmer]]s"|qq=}} were not considered ''chōnin''. Later, peasants, servants, and workers were also considered members of the social class. While ''chōnin'' are not as well known to non-Japanese as other social classes in Japan, they played a key role in the development of Japanese cultural products such as ''[[ukiyo-e]]'', ''[[rakugo]]'', and handicrafts. Aesthetic ideals such as [[:en:Iki (aesthetic ideal)|''iki'']], [[Iki (aesthetic ideal)#tsū|''tsū'']], and ''[[wabi-sabi]]'' were also developed among the ''chōnin''. This association with cultural development emerged as a way for members of the class to break the strict social barriers that prevented individuals from ascending in the social hierarchy. Members of the ''chōnin'' opted to develop culture within their communities, allowing members of such community to rise as "cultured individuals". This phenomenon is said to be behind the popularity of the ''{{ill|Iemoto|en|Iemoto|qid=Q2404873|lt=Iemoto|1=Iemoto}}'' system in the Edo period. The socioeconomic ascendance of ''chōnin'' has certain similarities to the roughly contemporary rise of the "{{ill|Bourgeoisie|en|Bourgeoisie|qid=Q52090|lt=Bourgeoisie|1=Bourgeoisie}}" in the West. In the latter part of the Tokugawa period, this social class wielded the real power in the society although the warrior class still dominated the political sphere.{{Cite book|title=Japan's Feet of Clay, Volume IX|last=Utley|first=Freda|date=2000|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=0415218241|location=London|pages=224|qq=}} From the mid-Edo period, wealthy ''chōnin'' and farmers could join the samurai class by giving a large sum of money to an impoverished {{nihongo3||御家人|{{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|qid=Q63765|lt=Gokenin|1=Gokenin}}|qq=}} to be adopted into a samurai family and inherit the samurai's position and stipend. The amount of money given to a ''gokenin'' varied according to his position: 1,000 ''ryo'' for a {{nihongo3|||{{ill|yoriki|en|yoriki|qid=Q3108649|lt=yoriki|1=Yoriki}}|qq=}} and 500 ''ryo'' for an {{nihongo3||[[:ja:徒士|徒士]]|kachi|qq=}}.{{cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BE%A1%E5%AE%B6%E4%BA%BA%E6%A0%AA-500197|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240716005616/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%BE%A1%E5%AE%B6%E4%BA%BA%E6%A0%AA-500197|script-title=ja:御家人株|language=ja|publisher=Kotobank|date=|archive-date=16 July 2024|access-date=16 July 2024|qq=}} Some of their descendants were promoted to {{nihongo3||旗本|{{ill|Hatamoto|en|Hatamoto|qid=Q572662|lt=Hatamoto|1=Hatamoto}}|qq=}} and held important positions in the shogunate.{{cite web|url=https://imidas.jp/jidaigeki/detail/L-57-dd0-08-04-G252.html|script-title=ja:武士（ぶし）／侍（さむらい）|language=ja|publisher=[[Shūeisha]]|date=|access-date=19 July 2024|qq=}} Low-Ranking samurai (''kachi'') could change jobs and move into the lower classes, such as ''chōnin''. For example, {{ill|Takizawa Bakin|en|Takizawa Bakin|qid=Q463142|lt=Takizawa Bakin|1=Takizawa Bakin}} became a ''chōnin'' by working for {{ill|Tsutaya Jūzaburō|en|Tsutaya Jūzaburō|qid=Q3541685|lt=Tsutaya Jūzaburō|1=Tsutaya Jūzaburō}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.cf.ocha.ac.jp/ccjs/j/menu/consortia/d007975_d/fil/121003.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240716005713/https://www.cf.ocha.ac.jp/ccjs/j/menu/consortia/d007975_d/fil/121003.pdf|script-title=ja:近世後期の江戸における武家の養子と身分 滝沢馬琴を事例に|language=ja|publisher=[[Ochanomizu University]]|date=|archive-date=16 July 2024|access-date=16 July 2024|qq=}} == Origins == [[File:Edo Hibachi.JPG|thumb|The house of the merchant (Fukagawa Edo Museum)]] By the late 17th century the prosperity and growth of {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} had begun to produce unforeseen changes in the Tokugawa social order. The ''chōnin'', who were theoretically at the bottom of the Edo hierarchy ([[:en:Four divisions of society|''shinōkōshō'']], samurai-farmers-craftsmen-merchants, with ''chōnin'' encompassing the two latter groups), flourished socially and economically at the expense of the ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]s'' and {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}}, who were eager to trade {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|qid=Q5090|lt=Rice|1=Rice}} (the principal source of domainal income) for cash and consumer goods. ==References== {{Reflist|qq=}} == External links == *[http://dostoevskiansmiles.blogspot.com/2008/10/on-nature-of-desideratum.html Samurai, Chōnin and the Bakufu: Between Cultures of Frivolity and Frugality.] *[http://www.britannica.com/eb/article-9082333 Britannica Article] <{{japan-hist-stub|qq=}} [[en:Chōnin]] [[ca:Chonin]] [[el:Τσόνιν]] [[es:Chōnin]] [[eu:Txonin]] [[fa:چونین]] [[fi:Chōnin]] [[fr:Chōnin]] [[hu:Csónin]] [[id:Chōnin]] [[it:Chōnin]] [[ja:町人]] [[ko:조닌]] [[ms:Chōnin]] [[pt:Chonin]] [[ro:Orășeanul Japoniei medievale]] [[ru:Тёнин]] [[tr:Chōnin]] [[zh:町人]] {{wikidata link|Q115847}} [[Category:Edo period]] [[Category:都市共同体]]
